title_en: Al-Hidayah Sharh Bidayat al-Mubtadi: The Gateway Commentary
title_ar: الهداية شرح بداية المبتدي: الشرح المدخلي الكبير
content_en:
Al-Hidayah fi Sharh Bidayat al-Mubtadi (The Guidance: A Commentary on The Beginning for the Student) by Burhan ad-Din Ali ibn Abi Bakr al-Marghinani (511–593 AH / 1135–1197 CE) is the most authoritative and widely used text in the Hanafi school. Written as a commentary on al-Marghinani's own primer Bidayat al-Mubtadi, Al-Hidayah provides the explanations, evidential basis, and legal reasoning that make the primer's concise rulings intelligible and applicable.

The relationship between Al-Hidayah and Bidayat al-Mubtadi is unique in the Islamic legal tradition: the same scholar wrote both the primer and its commentary, meaning that the commentary reflects an authoritative interpretation of the original text rather than a later scholar's reading of another's work. Al-Marghinani knew exactly what he intended by each ruling in Bidayat al-Mubtadi and explains it in Al-Hidayah with the authority of the original author.

Al-Hidayah's organization follows Bidayat al-Mubtadi's sequence through the standard fiqh topics. For each ruling in the primer, Al-Hidayah provides: the Hanafi school's evidential basis (Quranic verse, prophetic hadith, or the opinion of the school's founding imams); an explanation of the ruling's meaning and application; and, frequently, a comparison with the positions of other schools. This comparative dimension — engaging with the Shafi'i and Maliki schools in particular — makes Al-Hidayah not only a Hanafi legal guide but an introduction to comparative Islamic jurisprudence.

The evidential dimension of Al-Hidayah was innovative in its time. Earlier Hanafi manuals — including the works of ash-Shaybani from which Bidayat al-Mubtadi draws — typically stated rulings without their evidential basis, assuming that students would receive the evidence through oral teaching. Al-Hidayah incorporates the evidence directly into the text, making the school's positions more transparent and defensible in scholarly debate.

Al-Hidayah was translated into English by Charles Hamilton in 1791 — one of the earliest translations of an Islamic legal text into a European language — and this translation influenced the administration of Islamic law in British India for several decades. A more reliable modern translation was produced by Imran Nyazee in the twentieth century. These translations attest to the work's importance not only within the Islamic scholarly world but in the broader history of Islamic law's encounter with modernity.

The continuing centrality of Al-Hidayah in Hanafi legal education — from the madrasas of South Asia to the religious universities of Central Asia and Turkey — reflects its unique combination of authority, accessibility, and comparative breadth. Students who master Al-Hidayah have a comprehensive map of Hanafi law, a grounding in its evidential basis, and an awareness of how it relates to the positions of other schools.
